title: Symmetry breaking and high-dimensional chaos in sparse random networks of exact firing rate models
abstract: Exact firing rate models, also known as next-generation neural mass models (NG-NMMs), provide a rigorous description of the dynamics of neural populations. While in its simplest form a single population only displays fixed-point activity, multi-population models may display a range of different behaviors. In this work, we study the dynamics of all-excitatory or all-inhibitory NG-NMMs coupled through sparse random networks with row-normalized network topology. Linear stability analysis of the homogeneous states of the system, representing asynchronous neural activity, provides a dispersion relation linking the emergence of spatiotemporal dynamics to the spectra of the connectivity matrix. Using bounds from random matrix theory, we identify the parameter regions where instabilities occur. In undirected networks, only inhibitory systems produce heterogeneous stationary patterns, corresponding to a winner-takes-all mechanism. In directed networks, exotic rhythmic states with high frequencies emerge in both, excitatory and inhibitory systems. Numerical simulations reveal that these hectic oscillatory states correspond to high-dimensional chaos with extensive properties.
summary: This work analyzes the collective dynamics of neural networks modeled by QIF neurons coupled on sparse random graphs. By combining linear stability analysis with random matrix theory, the authors predict the onset of instabilities, which manifest as high-dimensional chaos in directed networks. The findings establish a theoretical link between network topology, coupling parameters, and the emergence of complex, chaotic spatiotemporal activity.
main_problem: The study investigates the emergence of spatiotemporal patterns and high-dimensional chaos in coupled neural populations modeled by exact firing rate models on sparse random networks.
main_result: The system exhibits extensive high-dimensional chaos in directed networks for both excitatory and inhibitory coupling, while undirected inhibitory networks show winner-takes-all steady states.
method: The analysis combines linear stability analysis of homogeneous states with random matrix theory to determine bifurcation boundaries, followed by Lyapunov exponent calculations to quantify chaos.
model_or_system: The model uses $N$ populations of Quadratic Integrate-and-Fire (QIF) neurons coupled via a sparse random network with row-normalized topology. The dynamics are governed by coupled differential equations.
key_observables: Lyapunov exponents ($\ell_i$), Kaplan-Yorke fractal dimension ($D_{KY}$), and the dispersion relation linking growth rates to connectivity matrix eigenvalues ($\Lambda_k$).
important_parameters: Coupling strength ($J$), network sparsity ($\kappa$), and the diagonal coupling parameter ($\mu$).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the system can be linearized around homogeneous states and utilizes the row-normalization constraint on the connectivity matrix.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate bifurcation diagrams showing instability boundaries, and others map the dynamical landscape using Lyapunov exponents and the scaling of the fractal dimension with system size.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from defining the QIF model and performing linear stability analysis to deriving spectral properties using random matrix theory, and finally characterizing the resulting dynamics using Lyapunov exponents to confirm high-dimensional chaos.
why_interesting: The rigorous mathematical framework for analyzing collective dynamics in large, sparse, and disordered systems is highly relevant to understanding complex quantum many-body systems where local interactions lead to emergent global behavior.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
